skill.name: using-git-worktrees
skill.description: Create isolated Git worktrees for feature work while following project directory conventions and baseline safety checks.
skill.summary: Set up isolated Git worktrees with directory selection, ignore checks, setup commands, and baseline test verification.
skill.author: ZhanlinCui

security_audit.summary: The static findings are mostly false positives from Markdown shell examples and literal path documentation. The skill is legitimate Git workflow guidance, but it instructs agents to run dependency installers, builds, tests, create worktrees, edit .gitignore, and commit changes, so marketplace publication should include a warning about command execution and repository modification.

medium_findings.title: Automatic Project Command Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run package installation, build, and test commands after creating a worktree. These are normal developer workflow commands, but commands such as npm install and project tests may execute repository-controlled scripts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly documented and relevant to the skill purpose. The risk comes from running project-controlled setup and test hooks, not from malicious code in this skill.

medium_findings.title: Repository Modification Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill tells agents to add an ignore rule and commit it when a project-local worktree directory is not ignored. This is a legitimate safety step, but it changes repository state and should be confirmed in sensitive repositories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file clearly instructs repository modification and committing. The action is transparent and safety-oriented, so the concern is operational rather than malicious.

low_findings.title: Static Shell Pattern Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The analyzer reported Ruby backtick execution, shell substitution, and template substitution across Markdown examples. These lines are fenced examples for Git worktree setup, not executable skill code, and most are false positives.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced content is Markdown guidance and examples. No Ruby source file or hidden executable wrapper is present in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: Hidden Path References Are Worktree Locations
low_findings.description: The analyzer flagged .worktrees and ~/.config paths as hidden file access. The references document possible worktree directories and do not access secrets or sensitive hidden files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The paths are shown as workspace destinations. I found no evidence of reading credentials, shell profiles, SSH keys, or other sensitive hidden files.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The analyzer reported weak cryptographic algorithm blockers at the description and integration lines. I found no cryptographic operation, hash selection, encryption routine, or credential handling in those lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain natural language about using the skill and another skill name. No evidence found for MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or another weak crypto primitive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Package Installers and Build Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The setup section lists npm install, cargo build, pip install, poetry install, and go mod download. These commands may execute dependency hooks or repository build logic.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are plainly present and can execute project-controlled code in common ecosystems. The surrounding guidance indicates legitimate setup intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Git Worktree Creation and Directory Change
dangerous_patterns.description: The creation step runs git worktree add with a branch name and then changes into the new path. This is expected for the skill but affects the filesystem and working directory.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is directly documented and matches the declared feature. The risk is bounded to normal Git and filesystem side effects.

content.value_statement: Feature work can disrupt an active branch and hide baseline test failures. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through isolated Git worktree setup with directory checks and test verification.
actual_capabilities: Selects an existing .worktrees or worktrees directory when available.
Checks CLAUDE.md for project-specific worktree directory guidance.
Prompts the user when no worktree location is established.
Verifies project-local worktree directories are ignored by Git.
Creates a new Git worktree and branch for isolated implementation.
Runs detected project setup and baseline test commands.
limitations: Requires a Git repository that supports git worktree commands.
Dependency installation and tests can run project-controlled scripts.
Does not resolve failing baseline tests without user direction.
Does not choose a directory automatically when project policy is unclear.

use_cases.title: Start Feature Work Safely
use_cases.description: Create a clean workspace before implementing a feature, while preserving the current branch state.

use_cases.title: Prepare Agent Implementation
use_cases.description: Give an AI coding agent a repeatable setup path before it edits code or runs tests.

use_cases.title: Standardize Repository Workflow
use_cases.description: Use consistent worktree locations, ignore rules, and baseline checks across a development team.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Worktree
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the using-git-worktrees skill to create an isolated worktree for my next feature branch.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for new feature work

prompt_templates.title: Follow Project Conventions
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up a Git worktree for feature/auth. Check existing worktree directories and CLAUDE.md before choosing a location.

prompt_templates.title: Verify Baseline Health
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a worktree for the refactor branch, install detected dependencies, run the relevant tests, and report any baseline failures before implementation.

prompt_templates.title: Handle Ambiguous Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare an isolated worktree for a risky change. If the worktree directory is not ignored or tests fail, stop and ask before continuing.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow with explicit safety checkpoints

output_examples.output: I found an existing .worktrees directory, verified it is ignored, created the feature/auth worktree, and ran the project tests.

output_examples.input: Set up a branch for the payment refactor.
output_examples.output: No worktree directory preference was found. I would ask whether to use a project-local .worktrees directory or the configured global location.

output_examples.input: Prepare a clean workspace before implementation.
output: Worktree location selected from project conventions.
Setup commands and baseline tests reported before coding starts.

best_practices: Confirm worktree directories are ignored before creating project-local worktrees.
Run baseline tests before implementation so existing failures are visible.
Ask the user before proceeding when setup policy or test status is unclear.
anti_patterns: Creating a worktree in an unignored project directory.
Running implementation work before checking baseline test status.
Assuming a global worktree location when the project already has a convention.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps an AI coding agent create and verify isolated Git worktrees before feature work begins.

faq.question: Does it modify the repository?
faq.answer: It can create a worktree, create a branch, and may add an ignore rule when required.

faq.question: Why does it check .gitignore?
faq.answer: The check helps prevent nested worktree contents from being accidentally tracked by the main repository.

faq.question: Does it run commands automatically?
faq.answer: The guidance includes setup and test commands, so users should review command execution in untrusted repositories.

faq.question: Can it work outside the project directory?
faq.answer: Yes. It supports a global worktree location under the user's configuration directory.

faq.question: Is this safe for marketplace publishing?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium-risk warning because it runs developer commands and changes repository state.
